1.exposition: A second angel descends, so freshly come from the presence of God that his glory floods the whole earth, and with a mighty voice he raises an ancient dirge: Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great. The words are not John's invention but Isaiah's over the first Babylon (Isaiah 21:9), and the chapter that follows is a mosaic of the Old Testament oracles against the great cities — Babylon in Jeremiah, Tyre in Ezekiel — laid now over imperial Rome. Three counts ground the sentence: the nations she made drunk, the kings she seduced, the merchants she enriched (v.3). Then a second heavenly voice turns from the doomed city to God's people still inside her: Come out of her, my people — separate, lest you share her sins and be caught in her plagues. Her guilt has piled heaven-high (v.5); the demand is retribution doubled (v.6); and her proud boast, I sit a queen, is answered in a single day of death, mourning, famine, and fire, for mighty is the Lord God who judges her (v.8).
1.original_language: The proclamation opens on a doubled prophetic aorist — epesen, epesen (“fell, fell”) — repeated, as Robertson notes, like a solemn dirge of the damned, the very cadence of 14:8 and Isaiah 21:9. The merchants grew rich by her strēnos (v.3) — not mere wealth but wanton, insolent luxury, self-indulgence swollen into arrogance. The summons of v.4, exelthate ho laos mou (“Come out, my people”), is an urgent second-aorist imperative, the same call the Septuagint puts on Israel's lips at the first Babylon (Isaiah 48:20; Jeremiah 51:45).
1.historical_context: The angel whose glory lights the earth echoes the divine kābôd that filled Ezekiel's temple (Ezekiel 43:2). But the sharpest historical edge is the boast of v.7 — “I sit a queen; I am no widow; I shall see no sorrow.” Rome minted her own permanence: Roma Aeterna on the coinage, the goddess Roma enthroned and triumphant, the poets singing an empire without end. John lifts the taunt-song of old Babylon (Isaiah 47:7–9) and of Tyre (Ezekiel 28:2) and turns it against the city on seven hills. Against centuries of imperial power he sets the refrain “in one day” (v.8): what seemed eternal is undone in an instant. The “come out of her” of v.4 reads successive empires through the same doom-texts — an established Jewish literary practice — so that Isaiah's and Jeremiah's Babylon becomes a template for Rome, and for every Babylon after.
1.christ: “Mighty is the Lord God who judged her” (v.8) grounds the whole sentence in the character of the judging God — and in Revelation that God is never separable from the Lamb who was slain and who “freed us from our sins by his blood” (1:5). The one who absorbed Babylon's violence in his own death is the one before whose court she now stands. This is why the call “Come out of her, my people” is a gospel word and not merely a warning: it is the church's Exodus, mirroring Israel's rescue from Egypt and from the literal Babylon — a summons to transfer one's whole allegiance from the condemned system to the Lamb before the judgment falls.

verses.note: A new herald appears, distinct from the angel-guide of the harlot vision, and the earth is lightened with his glory. latin-fathers Bede reads the mighty, light-bearing angel as the incarnate Lord himself, or the teachers of the Church “endued with heavenly light,” announcing the end of the world-city. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark him as an angel of the higher order, so recently come from the divine Presence that, in Swete's phrase, “he flings a broad belt of light across the dark earth” — the splendor of the messenger befitting the greatness of the doom he brings.

The cry itself: Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the doubled prophetic aorist as a dirge, borrowed word for word from 14:8 and behind it Isaiah 21:9. The ruined city becomes “a habitation of demons, a haunt of every foul spirit,” language Barnes traces to the desolation of the old Babylon (Isaiah 13:21); the evil spirits, Robertson adds, wait over fallen Rome “like night-birds that haunt the broken towers.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley numbers the horrid tenants of the desolate city — devils, unclean spirits, filthy and hateful birds — where once all uncleanness was practiced.

The threefold ground of judgment: the nations she made drunk, the kings she seduced, and the merchants waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies. grammatical-historical Ellicott sees her as an enemy of mankind in three aspects at once — she brought delirium on nations, reduced kings, and bribed merchants. Robertson notes the rare word strēnos (wanton luxury) and observes that here, uniquely, we catch a glimpse of the vast east-west traffic that made Rome rich; the merchants' coming lament (vv.11–19) follows straight from their dependence on her trade.

A second voice from heaven — the voice, Barnes judges, of God himself, since the hearers are called “my people” — cries Come out of her, my people. grammatical-historical Barnes gives the double reason: that they not partake of her sins, and that they not share her plagues, the summons echoing Isaiah 48:20, Jeremiah 51:45, and 2 Corinthians 6:17. Ellicott cautions that the “coming forth” is not chiefly a bodily exodus but the refusal of all fellowship with the world-spirit; latin-fathers Bede sets Lot's departure from Sodom as the pattern — the righteous must leave before the fire.

The reason her cup is full: her sins have reached unto heaven. grammatical-historical Ellicott pictures a great heap of guilt, firmly cemented and towering like another Babel toward the face of heaven (Genesis 11:4); Robertson notes the verb ekollēthēsan — the sins “glued together” in a mass mounting up (Jeremiah 51:9). And “God hath remembered her iniquities” — Barnes: he who in his long-suffering had seemed to forget now acts as though every sin were freshly before him.

The sentence of just recompense: reward her as she rewarded, and double unto her double. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the lex talionis raised to full measure — “she hath received of the Lord's hand double for all her sins” (Isaiah 40:2) — the cup she filled with corruption filled back to her doubled (Jeremiah 50:29). wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears God addressing the executioners of his vengeance, and takes the “double,” by the Hebrew idiom, to mean not twice her sins but a full and complete retaliation. latin-fathers Bede turns it to the eternal scale: she who reveled in temporal delights is racked with lasting torment.

Her pride sets the measure of her pain: as much as she glorified herself and lived deliciously, so much torment is given her, for she says in her heart, “I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow.” grammatical-historical Ellicott hears the boast of old Babylon (Isaiah 47:7–9) and of Tyre (Ezekiel 28:2) revived on Rome's lips. The false security is itself the height of the sin; Barnes marks it as the temper of a city that fears nothing even as its last judgments gather.

Therefore, in a single stroke, her plagues come in one day — death, mourning, famine, and utter burning. grammatical-historical Robertson takes “in one day” as the symbol of sheer suddenness, drawn again from Isaiah 47; the fire recalls the harlot's end in 17:16. The clause on which everything rests is the last: “strong is the Lord God who judged her.” Barnes grounds the certainty of the ruin not in Rome's weakness but in God's might — against the Almighty her boasted permanence is nothing.

witnesses.point: The light-bearing angel is the incarnate Lord, or the Church's teachers; and “come out of her” follows the pattern of Lot leaving Sodom before the fire.
The call “come out of her” has a double reason — not to partake of her sins, and not to share her plagues; the certainty of her ruin rests on the might of the God who judges her.
The doubled aorist “fell, fell” is a prophetic dirge; the rare word strēnos (wanton luxury) opens a rare glimpse of the vast east-west traffic that enriched Rome.
The “double” recompense, by Hebrew idiom, means a full retaliation, not twice her sins; the desolate city is left to its horrid unclean tenants.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Babylon is the trans-historical world-system of idolatrous, seductive commerce and power; chapter 18 is a deliberate mosaic of the Old Testament judgment oracles (Isaiah 13; 21; 47; Jeremiah 50–51) transferred onto Rome, and the “come out” recalls Israel's exodus from the literal Babylon.

1.application: Babylon is never only Rome. She is any order that trades on our appetites and calls its arrangement permanent. The word “Come out of her, my people” asks not first for geography but for allegiance: to see clearly what we are entangled with, and to withdraw our worship before the reckoning we cannot see coming “in one day.”

9.exposition: Against the heavenly proclamation rises an earthly lament, and it comes in three waves — kings, merchants, mariners — each standing “afar off,” each crying “Alas, alas,” each marveling that ruin fell “in one hour.” The pattern is lifted almost whole from Ezekiel's dirge over Tyre (Ezekiel 27), where the same three ranks mourn the sunken trading city. The grief is nakedly self-interested. The merchants weep not for Babylon's victims but because no one buys their cargo any more, and a long manifest follows (vv.12–13) — gold and silver, gems and pearls, fine cloth and rare woods, spices and wine and grain, cattle and horses — Rome's actual import list. It ends, damningly, where the whole system was always heading: and slaves, and human souls. The kings mourn their lost pleasure, the merchants their lost market, the sea-captains their lost freight; none mourns the blood. The dirge exposes what the mourners loved.
9.original_language: The cargo-list climaxes on a chilling pair: sōmata (“bodies,” a common commercial term for slaves) kai psychas anthrōpōn (“and souls of men”). John lets the euphemism and the reality stand side by side — the trade that dealt in bodies was dealing in persons. The word placed last is placed for weight. The mariners' cry, “What city is like unto this great city!”, is the very lament Ezekiel puts in the mouths of Tyre's sailors (Ezekiel 27:32), and the refrain en miâ hōrâ (“in one hour,” vv.10, 17, 19) tolls the suddenness through the whole passage.
9.historical_context: The manifest of some twenty-eight wares is a precise inventory of the empire's luxury trade: gold and gems from the East, pearls from the Persian Gulf, silk carried overland from China, purple from Tyre, citron-wood and ivory and marble for the villas of the rich, spices and frankincense from Arabia, wine and oil and Egyptian grain. Rome consumed the produce of the world and enriched a merchant elite in the process. That is the economic critique the vision presses: an empire whose splendor rested on the extraction of everything — and everyone — that could be bought. The threefold mourning of kings, merchants, and sea-captains follows Ezekiel's lament over Tyre (Ezekiel 27:12–36), the paradigmatic commercial city laid waste; Rome is Tyre's heir. And by setting “human souls” at the end of the list, John names the moral rot at the base of the whole glittering economy.
9.christ: The refrain “in one hour” (vv.10, 17, 19) stands in deliberate contrast to the Lamb, who “shall reign forever and ever” (11:15). Babylon's power is measured in hours; the Lamb's reign in eternity. The system the mourners weep over is the one the martyrs refused — those who “loved not their lives even unto death” (12:11) — and its collapse is the delayed answer to their cry, “How long?” (6:10). Babylon lasted exactly as long as the Lamb permitted, and not one hour more. The just judgment of God is thus also the vindication of the slain.

verses.note: The first lament: the kings who committed fornication and lived deliciously with her bewail her when they see the smoke of her burning. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark them as the partners in her sin who now mourn her ruin from self-interest, the rising smoke (as at Sodom, Genesis 19:28) drawing their grief. puritan Matthew Henry sees to the root of it: they lament not their sin but their loss, for “the spirit of antichrist is a worldly spirit,” and they weep for the pleasures and profits now gone.

They mourn at a safe distance: standing afar off for fear of her torment, crying “Alas, alas, that great city! for in one hour is thy judgment come.” puritan Matthew Henry catches the cowardice of it — even Babylon's friends will stand back from her fall, unwilling to share the plagues they shared her sins. grammatical-historical Robertson hears in “one hour” the first sounding of the refrain of suddenness that will toll three times over the chapter.

The second lament: the merchants weep and mourn, for no man buyeth their merchandise any more. grammatical-historical Barnes ties their grief to v.3 — the traders enriched by her now bereft of their market. reformed Gill argues the very fact that their sorrow is for lost trade shows the merchandise is not merely literal but the whole traffic “by which multitudes have wickedly lived in wealth” off the sins and follies of mankind. Either way the mourning is for profit, not for sin.

The manifest begins: gold, silver, precious stones, pearls, fine linen, purple, silk, scarlet, citron-wood, ivory, costly wood, bronze, iron, and marble. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read it as the inventory of a city given wholly to opulence and display — precious metals, jewels, rare fabrics, and imported stone. It is, Robertson notes, the one place in the book where the sheer scale of Rome's luxury commerce is set down item by item, the “abundance of her delicacies” catalogued for the eye.

The list runs on — spices, perfumes, wine, oil, flour, wheat, cattle, sheep, horses, chariots — and then halts on its damning last entry: and slaves, and souls of men. grammatical-historical Barnes fixes on it: “the traffic in slaves — in human beings — seemed the most odious form of commerce,” and John reserves it for the climax. The Greek pairs sōmata (bodies) with psychas anthrōpōn (souls of men): the system that trafficked in every luxury trafficked also in persons, and that is the moral indictment beneath all the splendor.

A word turns directly upon the city: the fruits thy soul lusted after are departed, and thou shalt find them no more at all. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the “ripe fruits” as the choice, autumn luxuries she craved and gloried in, now stripped away; the emphatic “no more at all” marks the loss as total and beyond recovery — the objects of her appetite gone forever.

The merchants take up the posture of the kings: made rich by her, they stand afar off in fear, weeping and wailing. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill note the tell-tale clause “made rich by her” — their wealth had come from her trade (v.3), and so their tears fall for themselves, for a fortune ended, not for a city judged.

Their dirge: Alas, alas, that great city, clothed in fine linen, purple, and scarlet, decked with gold, precious stones, and pearls. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the merchants describe her in the very array of the harlot (17:4) — and in the very wares they had sold her. They mourn the vanished magnificence that had been their own livelihood, her splendor recalled precisely in the hour of its ruin.

The marvel of it: in one hour so great riches is come to nought — and now the third rank appears, every shipmaster and sailor and sea-trader, standing afar off. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the refrain of suddenness sounding again, and Barnes and Gill mark the mariners as the last of the three mourning groups, the seafarers who carried her commerce and now watch their trade vanish with her.

Seeing the smoke, they cry What city is like unto this great city! grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson recognize the lament of Tyre's sailors reborn (Ezekiel 27:32): they mourn her as without equal, astonished that so incomparable a city could perish. The very praise is a measure of how completely the world's imagination had been captured by her.

The third lament reaches its climax: they cast dust on their heads, crying, Alas, alas, that great city — in one hour is she made desolate. grammatical-historical Barnes traces the mourning gesture to Ezekiel's Tyre (Ezekiel 27:30) and to Lamentations 2:10. Ellicott notes their reason, bald as ever — she was the city “wherein were made rich all that had ships in the sea by reason of her costliness.” They grieve the wealth, and the refrain “in one hour” seals the swiftness of its end.

witnesses.point: The cargo-list ends in slaves and “souls of men” — the traffic in human beings, “the most odious form of commerce,” set as the climax of the whole inventory.
The manifest is the one place the sheer scale of Rome's luxury trade is set down item by item; the refrain “in one hour” tolls the suddenness three times.
The mourners weep for their loss, not their sin; even Babylon's friends stand afar off from her fall, for “the spirit of antichrist is a worldly spirit.”
That the merchants grieve only for lost trade shows the merchandise stands for the whole traffic by which many “wickedly lived in wealth” off the sins of mankind.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The cargo-list is a precise catalogue of Rome's luxury imports, and by placing “bodies and human souls” last — the slave trade — John exposes the entire imperial economy as one that consumes people; the threefold lament of kings, merchants, and sea-captains follows Ezekiel's Tyre dirge and unmasks the mourners as those who profited, grieving only their vanished trade.

9.application: Read the manifest slowly and it stops being ancient. Every economy has a list, and the question is what sits at the end of it — whose body, whose soul, is quietly priced in so that the goods can flow. The mourners of Babylon are a warning against a grief that weeps only for lost comfort. Where our own comfort rests on someone's dehumanization, the gospel asks us to mourn the injustice, not the inconvenience.

20.exposition: To the threefold earthly lament heaven answers with a single summons: Rejoice over her, O heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets, for God hath avenged you on her. Where the kings, merchants, and mariners wept, the saints, apostles, and prophets are told to rejoice — the same three ranks, reversed. Then a mighty angel performs a prophetic sign, taking up a great millstone and hurling it into the sea: so shall Babylon be thrown down, and found no more at all. It is the very deed Jeremiah commanded Seraiah at the Euphrates (Jeremiah 51:63–64) — a scroll of doom bound to a stone and sunk. A litany of silences follows (vv.22–23): no music, no craftsman, no grinding mill, no lamplight, no voice of bride and bridegroom — every sound that makes a city alive, hushed. And the final verdict names the deepest charge: in her was found the blood of prophets and of saints, and of all slain upon the earth. The city that silenced the witnesses is itself silenced forever.
20.original_language: Five times over the litany of desolation repeats the emphatic double negative ou mē … eti — “shall be heard no more at all,” “found no more at all,” “shine no more at all” — hammering the finality of the ruin. The angel's word over the millstone, houtōs (“thus, in just this way”), makes the sinking stone a sign that interprets itself: as the stone vanishes, so vanishes the city. The verdict of v.23 traces her deceptions to her pharmakeia (“sorcery,” the root of “pharmacy”) — the drugged enchantment by which she seduced the nations, not crude magic but the intoxicating allure the whole chapter has described.
20.historical_context: The millstone hurled into the sea enacts the prophetic sign-act of Jeremiah 51:63–64, where Seraiah is told to tie the doom-oracle to a stone and cast it into the Euphrates, the sinking figuring Babylon's irreversible fall. The catalogue of silences draws on Jeremiah 25:10 and Jeremiah 7:34 — the stilling of music, millstone, lamp, and the voice of bride and groom that Jeremiah pronounced over Jerusalem's desolation, now spoken over Rome. Each item is a sound of ordinary life: the harpist, the artisan at his bench, the mill grinding grain, the lamp lit at evening, the wedding song. The vision strips away, one by one, the everyday goods that empire promised would never end. And the closing indictment — the blood of prophets and saints found in her — echoes Jesus' charge against Jerusalem (Matthew 23:35), fixing on Rome the guilt of the persecutor.
20.christ: “God hath avenged you on her” (v.20) is a legal verdict, and it is pronounced for the saints, apostles, and prophets — the court of the Lamb overturning the court of the beast. In the Johannine frame, the one who judges is the Son to whom the Father “has given all judgment” (John 5:22), so the saints' rejoicing is not private vengeance but the joy of seeing wrong finally named as wrong. The sea that swallows Babylon's stone (v.21) is the sea from which the beast first rose (13:1); the deep gives back its monster's city. And the “blood of prophets and saints” found in her (v.24) is the very blood whose avenging the martyrs begged (6:10) — so that heaven's coming “Alleluia” (19:1–2) is the answer to their cry.

verses.note: The heavenly counter-summons: Rejoice over her, thou heaven, and ye holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson set it in pointed contrast to the earthly lament — heaven rejoices exactly where the world mourns. puritan Matthew Henry grounds the joy not in cruelty but in justice displayed: God has taken up the cause of the persecuted and executed it, answering the martyrs' plea (6:10) and the bloodguilt named in v.24. The rejoicing is the vindication of God's people.

The prophetic sign: a mighty angel takes up a stone like a great millstone and casts it into the sea, declaring that thus shall Babylon be thrown down and found no more. grammatical-historical Robertson identifies the enacted symbol of Jeremiah 51:63–64. Barnes draws the point: she will be “cast down with violence, suddenly and with power,” and sink utterly, “as a millstone thrown into the deep sea sinks at once, and is seen no more.” The finality is absolute — she vanishes.

The silences begin: no voice of harpers or musicians, no craftsman of any craft, no sound of a millstone, heard in thee any more. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace the language to Jeremiah 25:10 and Ezekiel 26:13: the music of festivity, the labor of the artisan, the daily grinding of grain — all stilled at once. It is the portrait of a city drained of both its joy and its work, wholly dead.

Darkness and the end of gladness: the light of a candle shall shine no more, and the voice of bridegroom and bride be heard no more; for her merchants were the great men of the earth, and by her sorceries were all nations deceived. grammatical-historical Ellicott and Barnes note her former greatness recalled — her traders were the world's magnates — even as the true ground is named: her pharmakeia, the enchantment by which she seduced the nations (Jeremiah 25:10). Lamp and wedding-song, the last tokens of a living home, go out together.

The final and gravest charge: in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson name her the great persecutor, in whom the blood of God's martyred people is found (17:6). Ellicott and Barnes see her comprehensive guilt charged as Jerusalem's was (Matthew 23:35) — the whole weight of shed innocent blood laid to the account of the city that made shedding it her policy. This is the deepest reason her fall is cause for heaven's joy.

witnesses.point: The millstone shows her cast down “suddenly and with power,” to sink and be seen no more; her final and gravest charge is the blood of the prophets and saints found in her.
The hurled millstone enacts the sign of Jeremiah 51:63–64; heaven's call to rejoice stands in pointed contrast to the earthly lament of the three mourning ranks.
Heaven's rejoicing is grounded not in cruelty but in the justice of God displayed — the vindication of a people long persecuted, their cause at last taken up and answered.
Her deceptions are traced to her sorcery, the enchantment by which she seduced the nations; her bloodguilt is charged comprehensively, as Jerusalem's was in Matthew 23.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: The millstone is a prophetic sign-act drawn from Jeremiah 51, and the fivefold litany of silences reverses the ordinary sounds of a living city — music, craft, grinding, lamplight, weddings — so that the empire which silenced the prophets is itself silenced; the summons to rejoice is judicial vindication of the wronged, not mere vindictiveness.

20.application: The hardest note in the chapter is the command to rejoice at a fall. It is bearable only because the joy is not our own settling of scores but heaven's: “God hath avenged you.” The saints do not celebrate suffering; they celebrate that wrong is finally named as wrong by a court that cannot be bribed. To rejoice rightly is to hand our grievances to that court — and to be sure that the blood empire spills is never, in the end, forgotten.

_meta.title: The Dirge over Fallen Babylon
reflection: The one voice of grace in the chapter says, “Come out of her, my people” (v.4). Babylon is not only Rome but any system that seduces with wealth while crushing the weak. Where is God calling you to a costly separation from a Babylon you quietly profit from?

Every mourner grieves his lost trade, and the cargo-list ends in “human souls” (v.13). What in your own economy of comfort depends on someone else's dehumanization — and would you mourn its loss, or its injustice?

Heaven is told to rejoice because “God hath avenged” the blood of the prophets and saints (v.20, v.24). How is the saints' joy over judgment different from vengeance — and what would it mean to trust God's court rather than settle your own accounts?
